title: Verifiable Delegation Primitive for AIP/MCP/A2A Interop
description: The AIP paper proposes verifiable delegation across MCP and A2A using DIDs and VCs but lacks a ZKP-based scope-narrowing mechanism. Bolyra's Delegation circuit enforces one-way scope narrowing on-chain, which could become the cryptographic backbone for AIP-compliant delegation chains where sub-delegated agents provably cannot escalate privileges.

rationale.demand: Web search evidence returned zero results and the AIP paper is a single academic preprint with no named buyers, procurement cycles, or funded competitors building AIP-compliant delegation specifically.
rationale.timing: AIP is a fresh arxiv paper with no published standard timeline, no competitor implementations, and no regulatory deadline — the 18+ month assessment from evidence gathering is accurate.
rationale.fit: Near-perfect primitive mapping: Delegation circuit already enforces one-way scope narrowing, AgentPolicy handles per-hop credential verification, and HumanUniqueness anchors the chain root — this is exactly what Bolyra was built for.
rationale.feasibility: SDK delegate() stub exists, circuits are proven, and the MVP spec is well-scoped at ~17 days, but promoting a stub to full implementation with 3-hop chain serialization and a new spec profile is non-trivial solo-founder work.

mapped_primitives.usage: Direct match — the Delegation circuit already enforces one-way scope narrowing (delegateeScope ⊂ delegatorScope), expiry narrowing, and chain linking up to 3 hops. AIP's verifiable delegation chains map almost 1:1 to this circuit's capabilities. The circuit becomes the ZKP enforcement layer beneath AIP's DID/VC delegation model.

Each agent in an AIP delegation chain needs to prove its credential is valid (operator-signed, unexpired, permissions sufficient). AgentPolicy already handles selective disclosure of permission bits against a requiredScopeMask, which maps to AIP's capability-based access control at each hop.

AIP delegation chains originate from a human principal. HumanUniqueness proves the root delegator is a unique human without revealing identity, satisfying AIP's requirement that delegation chains trace back to an accountable principal while preserving privacy.

On-chain delegation verification with chain-state tracking, delegation nullifier replay protection, and hop count enforcement already exist. The contract serves as the AIP-compatible verifiable registry where delegation proofs are settled and where any verifier can check chain validity.

SDK already exposes createAgentCredential, permissionsToBitmask, and validateCumulativeBitEncoding. The delegate() stub (v0.3) is the natural surface for AIP delegation — it needs to be promoted from stub to full implementation with AIP-compatible parameters.

AIP uses DIDs as agent identifiers. did:bolyra already defines a DID method tied to Bolyra credentials. AIP interop requires did:bolyra resolution to return delegationChain metadata in the DID Document, which is a spec extension but not a rewrite.

The IETF draft defines the mutual ZKP auth handshake. AIP delegation extends this to multi-hop scenarios — the draft needs a new section on delegation chain verification semantics and how they compose with MCP/A2A transport bindings.

new_components_needed.description: AIP Delegation Profile spec: maps Bolyra's Delegation circuit semantics to AIP's delegation model. Defines how scope-narrowing proofs embed in AIP's VC-based delegation tokens, how MCP tool_list and A2A AgentCard advertise delegated capabilities, and the verification algorithm for multi-hop chains across protocol boundaries.

MCP delegation transport binding: extends @bolyra/mcp to attach delegation proofs to MCP tool invocations. When a sub-agent calls an MCP tool on behalf of a delegator, the proof of scope-narrowing travels in the request metadata. Needs to handle the MCP sampling flow where a server delegates back to a client.

A2A delegation transport binding: maps Bolyra delegation proofs into Google A2A's AgentCard and Task metadata. Sub-delegated agents advertise their narrowed scope in the AgentCard skills field, and delegation proofs accompany Task creation/updates.

Promote delegate() from stub to full implementation. Needs to support chain construction (human→agent→sub-agent), serialization of delegation tokens with embedded ZK proofs, and verification of received delegation chains. Must enforce the 3-hop max and scope/expiry narrowing at the SDK level before circuit proving.

Conformance test vectors for delegation interop: extend the existing 37-vector suite with delegation chain scenarios — valid 2-hop and 3-hop chains, scope escalation rejection, expiry violation rejection, cross-protocol (MCP→A2A) delegation hand-off.

Optional: DelegationRegistry facet that stores delegation chain roots on-chain for persistent verification. Current IdentityRegistry tracks chain-state but may need a dedicated mapping for AIP-style long-lived delegation grants vs per-handshake ephemeral proofs.

positioning_alignment: AIP positions itself as a unifying framework over MCP and A2A but deliberately leaves the cryptographic enforcement layer unspecified — it relies on DIDs and VCs without defining how to prevent scope escalation in ZK. Bolyra slots in as the privacy-preserving enforcement layer beneath AIP-compliant orchestrators. This is textbook privacy-layer-under-incumbents: Google (A2A) and Anthropic (MCP) define the transport, AIP defines the interop semantics, and Bolyra provides the cryptographic guarantee that no agent in the chain can exceed its delegated authority — without revealing the full permission set or the delegator's identity. The strategy is to make Bolyra the default delegation verification primitive that AIP-compatible systems reference, similar to how Semaphore became the default for anonymous signaling. Early conformance vectors and reference implementations for both MCP and A2A transports create switching costs before alternatives emerge.

deliverables.name: AIP Delegation Profile spec
deliverables.description: One-document spec mapping Bolyra Delegation circuit semantics onto AIP's VC-based delegation model. Defines scope-narrowing proof embedding, hop-count semantics, and MCP/A2A transport binding points. Lives at spec/aip-delegation-profile.md

deliverables.name: SDK delegate() full implementation
deliverables.description: Promote delegate() from v0.3 stub to working API: createDelegationChain(parentCredential, childScope, expiry) → DelegationProof. Includes witness generation, proof creation (snarkjs), chain serialization up to 3 hops, and scope-narrowing validation. The heavy lift.

deliverables.name: SDK verifyDelegationChain()
deliverables.description: Verifier-side API: takes a serialized delegation chain, verifies each hop's ZKP, checks cumulative scope narrowing and expiry monotonicity. Returns typed result with hop-by-hop audit trail.

deliverables.name: did:bolyra delegationChain DID Document extension
deliverables.description: Add delegationChain service endpoint and verificationMethod entries to did:bolyra resolution. Minor spec delta — 1 new section in did-method-bolyra.md plus SDK resolver update.

deliverables.name: IdentityRegistry delegation event indexing
deliverables.description: Add DelegationVerified event emission and delegationNullifier replay check to existing contract. No new contract — extends current IdentityRegistry.

deliverables.name: 3-hop delegation chain integration test
deliverables.description: End-to-end test: Human → Agent A (full perms) → Agent B (read+write only) → Agent C (read only). Proves scope narrows at each hop, escalation fails, expired chains reject. Doubles as the demo artifact.
deliverables.reuses: existing circuit test harness + HumanUniqueness + AgentPolicy

deliverables.name: IETF draft §7 Delegation Chain Verification
deliverables.description: New section in draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 covering multi-hop delegation verification semantics, composability with MCP tool_list and A2A agent cards.

risks: AIP is an academic paper with no adopted standard body yet — building to a moving target that may diverge or stall
Web search evidence returned zero demand signal — no confirmed ecosystem pull for AIP-compatible delegation
3-hop chain verification gas costs on Base Sepolia may be prohibitive for real-world use without a batching/rollup layer
Solo founder capacity: 21 days is 4+ calendar weeks given context switching with other Bolyra/ZKProva/GeniusComply work
MCP and A2A transport bindings are underspecified in AIP — our spec may need revision once those protocols stabilize
mvp.success_criteria: The 3-hop integration test passes end-to-end: a human-rooted delegation chain with progressive scope narrowing creates valid ZKPs at each hop, the on-chain registry accepts and indexes them, escalation attempts are rejected by the circuit, and the SDK round-trips createDelegationChain → serialize → verifyDelegationChain with <5s proving time per hop using rapidsnark.

title: LangChain/CrewAI Agent Auth Middleware with ZKP Handshakes
description: LangChain's State of Agent Engineering highlights authentication as a production deployment concern. Bolyra already has a LangChain integration stub; shipping a credential middleware that lets LangChain agents prove identity via mutual ZKP handshake before tool invocation would capture the framework-native developer audience.

rationale.demand: Web search evidence failed entirely — zero URLs, zero named buyers, zero engagement metrics; the sole citation ('LangChain State of Agent Engineering') is unverified and provides no quantified demand signal.
rationale.timing: No competitor launch dates, no standard milestones, no regulatory deadlines were surfaced; timing argument is purely speculative with no dated anchors.
rationale.fit: All three core circuits (AgentPolicy, HumanUniqueness, Delegation) plus the SDK's proveHandshake/verifyHandshake map directly with no circuit modifications, and integration stubs already exist in the repo.
rationale.feasibility: Deliverables are thin integration wrappers over proven SDK primitives requiring ~13 days, but LangChain/CrewAI middleware API stability is unverified and no search evidence confirms the integration surface exists as described.

mapped_primitives.usage: Each LangChain/CrewAI agent proves its credential (operator signature, permission bitmask, expiry) before invoking tools. The selective disclosure capability lets agents prove they hold required permissions (e.g. FINANCIAL_SMALL) without revealing their full bitmask to the tool provider.

Human-in-the-loop workflows where a user delegates to an agent chain — the human proves enrollment once at session start, then agents carry derived authority. Also enables sybil-resistant rate limiting per human principal across agent invocations.

Multi-hop agent chains (user → orchestrator agent → sub-agent tool call) use the delegation circuit to prove scope narrowing at each hop. LangChain's agent-calls-agent pattern maps directly to max-3-hop delegation chains with bitwise scope subsetting.

On-chain verification endpoint for high-value tool calls (financial APIs, PII access). Atomic handshake verification gives tool providers a single-tx trust anchor. Root history buffers tolerate slight staleness from batched enrollments.

The SDK's proveHandshake/verifyHandshake functions become the core of the middleware — wrapped in LangChain's RunnablePassthrough or BaseTool.before_invoke pattern. Error classes (ExpiredCredentialError, ScopeEscalationError) map cleanly to LangChain's retry/fallback mechanisms.

new_components_needed.description: LangChain CallbackHandler or RunnableMiddleware that intercepts tool invocations, generates an AgentPolicy proof for the required permission scope, and attaches it to the tool call metadata. Verifier side validates proof before execution proceeds.
CrewAI tool decorator (@bolyra_auth) that wraps any CrewAI Tool with pre-invocation ZKP handshake, reading required permissions from tool metadata and failing fast on scope escalation.

Session credential cache — proving takes 16s (PLONK). Middleware needs a session-scoped proof cache keyed by (agent_id, scope, nonce_window) so repeat tool calls within a session don't re-prove. Invalidates on expiry or scope change.

Extension to draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 defining a 'framework binding' section: how proof payloads embed in framework-specific metadata (LangChain run_info, CrewAI task context), nonce lifecycle for multi-step chains, and scope escalation error semantics.

Example chains: (1) RAG chain with PII-gated retriever requiring bit 7, (2) financial agent chain requiring delegation from human with bit 4 narrowed to bit 2 at sub-agent, (3) multi-agent crew where each agent proves its own credential independently.

positioning_alignment: This is textbook privacy-layer-under-incumbents positioning. LangChain (40K+ GitHub stars) and CrewAI (25K+) own the orchestration layer but have zero cryptographic auth primitives — they punt to OAuth/API keys. Bolyra slots underneath as the trust layer these frameworks delegate to, without competing on orchestration. The middleware pattern means framework developers adopt Bolyra without switching orchestrators, and every LangChain/CrewAI deployment that adds Bolyra auth becomes a distribution channel. This mirrors Stripe's embed-in-every-framework strategy but for agent identity rather than payments.

deliverables.name: @bolyra/langchain-auth
deliverables.description: LangChain RunnableMiddleware that intercepts tool invocations, requires AgentPolicy proof with matching permission scope before execution proceeds. Wraps proveHandshake/verifyHandshake from @bolyra/sdk. Includes session-scoped proof cache keyed by (agent_id, scope, nonce_window) to avoid re-proving on repeat calls within a session.

deliverables.name: @bolyra/crewai-auth
deliverables.description: CrewAI tool decorator (@bolyra_auth) that reads required permissions from tool metadata, runs ZKP handshake pre-invocation, and raises ScopeEscalationError on insufficient scope. Thin wrapper over the same SDK primitives.

deliverables.name: Session proof cache
deliverables.description: In-memory LRU cache in @bolyra/sdk that stores valid proofs keyed by (agentId, scopeBitmask, nonceWindow). Auto-invalidates on expiry or scope change. Eliminates repeated 16s PLONK proving for same-session repeat tool calls.

deliverables.name: Framework binding spec section
deliverables.description: Extension to draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 Section 8: 'Framework Bindings' — defines how middleware intercepts tool calls, proof attachment format in tool metadata, cache semantics, and error codes for framework-native retry/fallback.

deliverables.name: Multi-hop delegation demo
deliverables.description: Working example: human enrolls → delegates to LangChain orchestrator agent → orchestrator sub-delegates to a CrewAI tool-calling agent → tool verifies narrowed scope. Demonstrates 3-hop delegation chain with bitwise scope subsetting. Published as examples/langchain-crewai-auth/.

deliverables.name: Integration docs + quickstart
deliverables.description: README with 5-line quickstart for each framework, architecture diagram, permission mapping guide (LangChain tool metadata → Bolyra bitmask), and troubleshooting for common proof failures.

risks: LangChain's middleware API is unstable — RunnablePassthrough pattern may change between 0.2.x and 0.3.x, forcing rewrites
16s PLONK proving latency even with caching makes real-time tool invocation awkward; developers may reject the UX unless rapidsnark brings it under 2s
CrewAI's decorator model has no formal pre-invoke hook — implementation may require monkey-patching internals rather than clean extension
Demand signal is weak (search evidence returned nothing) — this may be building ahead of actual developer pull
Session cache invalidation edge cases (concurrent agents, clock skew on expiry) could produce subtle auth bypass bugs if not carefully tested
mvp.success_criteria: 10+ GitHub stars on the integration package within 60 days of release, at least 3 external developers successfully running the multi-hop demo end-to-end (tracked via GitHub Discussions or issues), and the LangChain community hub or CrewAI docs linking to Bolyra as a supported auth middleware option.

title: Bolyra DID Method as Reference Implementation for Agent DIDs
description: Multiple signals (AgentGraph, AIP, Okta) converge on W3C DIDs for agent identity but none integrate ZKP-based resolution. Bolyra's did:bolyra method spec and conformance test vectors position it to become the reference DID method for privacy-preserving agent identity, especially as IETF and W3C working groups formalize agent authentication standards.
source: Convergence across AgentGraph, AIP, Okta signals

rationale.demand: Web search evidence returned zero results and no verifiable URLs were provided — demand rests on anecdotal convergence across AgentGraph/AIP/Okta without concrete engagement metrics or named buyers.
rationale.timing: No specific competitor launch dates, RFC draft versions, or W3C working group milestones were cited; the 18+ month timing assessment from evidence gathering contradicts the preliminary score of 21.
rationale.fit: Strong primitive mapping — all three circuits (AgentPolicy, HumanUniqueness, Delegation), the IdentityRegistry VDR, and the existing did:bolyra spec apply directly with only minor modifications needed.
rationale.feasibility: MVP spec failed to generate (CLI timeout), so there are no deliverables, timeline, or reuse estimates to validate; without a concrete build plan, feasibility cannot be scored above speculative.

mapped_primitives.usage: DID document resolution returns the agent's credential commitment and permission scope — AgentPolicy proves the agent holds a valid credential matching that DID without revealing the full bitmask or operator key. DID resolution becomes a ZKP verification step, not a lookup.

did:bolyra DIDs for human controllers use the Semaphore-based nullifier to prove the DID subject is a unique human without linking across scopes. Essential for the 'controller' field in agent DID documents — proves a real human authorized the agent without doxxing the human.

DID document metadata encodes delegation chains. The Delegation circuit proves scope narrowing is valid when resolving a delegated agent DID (e.g., did:bolyra:agent:sub-delegated-hash). The max-3-hop constraint maps directly to DID delegation depth limits in the spec.

On-chain registry serves as the verifiable data registry (VDR) for did:bolyra resolution. Dual Merkle trees provide the membership roots referenced in DID documents. Root history buffers enable resolution against recent-but-not-current roots, which is critical for DID resolution during tree update latency windows.

The existing DID method spec is the direct deliverable. Already defines the method-specific identifier syntax, CRUD operations, and resolution algorithm. Needs hardening for W3C DID Core 1.1 compliance and interop with Universal Resolver.

37 existing conformance vectors provide the foundation for a DID resolution test suite. Standards bodies weight reference implementations with test coverage heavily — this is a credibility multiplier.

The IETF draft defines the authentication handshake that DID-based interactions invoke. Agent DIDs resolved via did:bolyra trigger the mutual ZKP auth flow defined in the draft — the two specs are complementary and mutually reinforcing in standards discussions.

new_components_needed.description: DID Core 1.1 compliance gap analysis and spec update: add JSON-LD @context, implement deactivation semantics, define key rotation via DID document versioning tied to IdentityRegistry root history, and register did:bolyra with the W3C DID Method Registry.

Universal Resolver driver for did:bolyra — a Docker container that accepts DID resolution requests and returns DID documents by querying the IdentityRegistry contract on Base Sepolia. Required for interop with the DIF Universal Resolver infrastructure that AgentGraph and others will consume.

DID resolution functions in @bolyra/sdk: resolveDID(did) → DIDDocument, createDID(identity|credential) → did:bolyra:..., deactivateDID(). Thin wrappers over existing createHumanIdentity/createAgentCredential that produce W3C-compliant DID documents.

Agent DID profile specification: defines how did:bolyra encodes agent-specific metadata (permission scope, delegation depth, expiry) in DID document service endpoints and verification methods. This is the novel contribution no other DID method offers.

DIDComm v2 message envelope support: agents identified by did:bolyra need to send/receive DIDComm messages. Requires implementing DIDComm encryption/decryption using the Baby Jubjub keys already in the identity primitives, plus a service endpoint definition in the DID document.

positioning_alignment: This opportunity is the purest expression of the privacy-layer-under-incumbents strategy. AgentGraph, AIP, and Okta are all converging on DIDs for agent identity but none have ZKP resolution — they will resolve to plaintext documents. Bolyra positions did:bolyra as the privacy-preserving DID method that these frameworks reference when they need selective disclosure and sybil resistance. The Universal Resolver driver makes Bolyra consumable without vendor lock-in: any framework resolving a did:bolyra identifier gets ZKP verification transparently. Standards body participation (W3C DID WG, IETF) creates a moat that pure-product competitors cannot replicate — once did:bolyra is registered and test vectors are cited in working group drafts, switching costs for the ecosystem become structural. The existing 37 test vectors and IETF draft give Bolyra a 6-12 month head start over any competitor attempting to register a ZKP-based DID method.

risks: Claude CLI timed out after 240s

title: Privacy-Preserving Agent Identity Layer for Enterprise IdPs
description: Okta's Agent Identity platform treats agents as first-class identities but independent analysis notes gaps in zero-knowledge proof support and privacy-preserving credential delegation. Bolyra's AgentPolicy circuit and Delegation circuit could serve as a ZKP overlay that plugs into Okta/Entra ID token flows, letting enterprises prove agent permissions without exposing the full credential chain.
source: Okta Agent Identity launch + dev.to gap analysis

rationale.demand: Web search evidence returned zero results and no verifiable URLs were provided — demand is speculative based on a single Okta launch and one dev.to post, with no named buyers or procurement signals.
rationale.timing: No competitor launch dates, standard milestones, or regulatory deadlines were cited; Okta's agent identity platform is new but enterprise ZKP adoption has no concrete timeline anchors.
rationale.fit: AgentPolicy, Delegation, and HumanUniqueness map directly with minor modifications, and the SDK already exposes the needed primitives — this is a genuine extension of existing Bolyra capabilities.
rationale.feasibility: The MVP spec is reasonable at ~10 days but the OIDC-to-bitmask mapping introduces a new trust boundary and scope registry that needs careful design; no existing enterprise IdP integration code exists to build on.

mapped_primitives.usage: Core primitive for the overlay. Enterprise agents get Bolyra credentials with permission bitmasks derived from Okta app grants. The selective disclosure capability lets agents prove they hold required scopes (e.g. FINANCIAL_SMALL) to a relying party without revealing the full Okta token or permission set. The PLONK proving system avoids per-enterprise ceremony overhead.

Maps directly to Okta's delegated admin and service account patterns. When a human operator in Okta delegates a subset of their permissions to an agent, the Delegation circuit enforces scope narrowing and expiry narrowing on-chain — the enterprise IdP issues the broad grant, Bolyra proves the narrowed sub-grant without exposing the delegation chain. The 3-hop limit covers human→agent→sub-agent flows common in enterprise orchestration (e.g. manager→booking-agent→payment-agent).

Anchors the human operator at the root of every delegation chain. Enterprises need to know a real employee authorized the agent without necessarily revealing which employee. The nullifier scoping lets the same employee operate multiple agents across departments without cross-linking.

The dual-tree design (humanTree + agentTree) and atomic handshake verification already support the enterprise flow: register agent credentials in agentTree, verify human+agent handshakes atomically. Root history buffers handle the lag between Okta provisioning events and on-chain Merkle updates.

The existing createAgentCredential → proveHandshake → verifyHandshake pipeline is the programmatic surface enterprises would integrate. Error classes like ExpiredCredentialError and ScopeEscalationError map to enterprise audit requirements.

Provides the normative wire format for the ZKP handshake. Enterprise IdP integrations need a standards-track document to pass security review. The existing IETF draft positions Bolyra as interoperable rather than proprietary.

Gives each agent a resolvable DID that enterprise systems can reference in access logs and audit trails without revealing the underlying credential. Maps to Okta's agent-as-identity model with a privacy layer on top.

new_components_needed.description: OIDC/OAuth2 token bridge: a middleware that accepts an Okta (or Entra ID) access token, extracts the agent's scopes/roles, maps them to Bolyra's 8-bit permission bitmask, and calls createAgentCredential. This is the critical glue — it translates enterprise IdP semantics into Bolyra circuit inputs. Needs to handle token refresh, scope mapping configuration per tenant, and JWKS validation.

Enterprise scope mapping specification: a normative document defining how OAuth2 scopes and Okta app grants map to Bolyra's cumulative-bit permission model. Enterprises won't adopt without a deterministic, auditable mapping. Includes canonical mappings for common patterns (read-only, financial tiers, PII access) and an extension point for custom scopes.

SCIM provisioning adapter: listens to Okta SCIM events (agent created, deprovisioned, scope changed) and maintains the agentTree Merkle membership accordingly. Without this, enterprises must manually sync agent lifecycles — a non-starter for orgs with hundreds of agents.

Enterprise admin SDK surface: tenant configuration (scope mapping, tree management, audit log export), batch credential issuance, and revocation list sync. Wraps the existing SDK with multi-tenant isolation and key management hooks for enterprise HSM/KMS integration.

On-chain revocation registry or nullifier-based credential status list. Okta can revoke agent access instantly; the on-chain state must reflect this within the root history buffer window. Current IdentityRegistry handles Merkle removal but lacks an explicit revocation signal that relying parties can check without re-proving membership.

Credential status circuit: proves that an agent credential has NOT been revoked (non-membership in a revocation accumulator) without revealing which credential is being checked. Prevents the privacy leak where checking revocation status links the agent to a specific Okta identity. Could use a sparse Merkle tree non-membership proof.

positioning_alignment: This is the canonical instantiation of Bolyra's privacy-layer-under-incumbents strategy. Okta and Entra ID own the enterprise identity graph and will not be displaced. Bolyra does not compete with them — it sits underneath, converting their opaque token flows into ZKP-verifiable claims. The enterprise buys Okta for identity management and Bolyra for privacy-preserving proof of agent authority. This avoids the impossible fight of displacing enterprise IdPs while capturing the emerging requirement that agent actions be verifiable without full credential exposure. The IETF draft and DID method give procurement teams the standards-track cover they need. The risk is that Okta builds native ZKP support, but their incentive structure (token introspection revenue, audit log monetization) actively conflicts with privacy-preserving verification, making this a durable wedge.

deliverables.name: oidc-credential-mapper
deliverables.description: SDK module that takes a standard OIDC token (Okta/Entra ID), extracts scopes and claims, maps them to Bolyra 8-bit permission bitmask, and outputs a valid AgentCredential input struct. Pure function, no network calls — the bridge between enterprise IdP world and Bolyra circuit world.

deliverables.name: delegation-from-oauth-scope
deliverables.description: Helper that takes a broad OIDC scope set (e.g. payments.read, payments.write, admin.users) and a desired narrowed subset, produces a Delegation circuit input that enforces the narrowing. Maps OAuth scope strings to cumulative bit positions via a configurable scope registry.

deliverables.name: enterprise-handshake-example
deliverables.description: End-to-end Node.js example: (1) receive mock Okta JWT, (2) extract scopes, (3) create Bolyra agent credential, (4) prove AgentPolicy with PLONK (no ceremony), (5) verify. Ships with a fake Okta JWKS endpoint for local testing. README-driven, zero infra dependencies.

deliverables.name: oidc-binding-spec-appendix
deliverables.description: Appendix to draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 specifying: OIDC claim-to-bitmask mapping, token binding via nonce commitment (hash of OIDC at_hash into sessionNonce), and audit trail requirements. Normative enough for enterprise security review, short enough to ship fast.

deliverables.name: enterprise-integration-docs
deliverables.description: Technical guide: 'Plugging Bolyra into Okta/Entra ID'. Covers architecture diagram, scope mapping table, privacy guarantees (what the relying party learns vs doesn't), and threat model delta vs raw OIDC. Positioned as educational open-source content.

risks: Zero validated demand — web search returned nothing, feasibility scored 15/25. Enterprise ZKP-over-OIDC may be a solution looking for a problem.
Enterprise sales cycles are 6-18 months and require active selling (WAIT_FOR_EAD). Building the technical layer is permitted but you cannot close deals.
Okta or Microsoft could add native selective disclosure (SD-JWT is already in their roadmap) making the ZKP overlay redundant before you can sell it.
OIDC scope semantics vary wildly across enterprises — the scope-to-bitmask mapping may need per-tenant configuration that bloats the 'simple overlay' pitch.
No on-chain gas cost analysis for enterprise volumes — Merkle tree updates at enterprise provisioning scale (thousands of agents) may hit cost or latency walls on Base Sepolia.
mvp.success_criteria: The enterprise-handshake-example runs end-to-end in under 30 seconds on a laptop: mock Okta JWT in, verified ZKP handshake out, with the relying party learning only 'this agent has FINANCIAL_SMALL permission' and nothing else about the token or delegation chain. Secondary: at least 2 external developers (not you) can follow the docs and run the example without asking questions. Tertiary: the spec appendix is self-consistent with the existing IETF draft and passes a self-review checklist for normative language.

title: Privacy-Preserving Agent Purchase Authentication
description: Eco's agent identity verification for purchases uses DID-based identity with behavioral trust scoring. Bolyra's mutual handshake protocol (human proof + agent proof + nonce binding) could replace behavioral scoring with cryptographic proof that an agent is authorized by a verified human for a specific spending tier, without revealing the human's identity to the merchant.

rationale.demand: Web search evidence returned zero results and no verifiable URLs, named buyers, or independent sources were provided — demand is entirely speculative based on a single Eco article.
rationale.timing: No competitor launch dates, standard milestones, or regulatory deadlines were cited; agent commerce purchasing is still pre-market with no concrete timeline anchors beyond general industry interest.
rationale.fit: All three core circuits (HumanUniqueness, AgentPolicy, Delegation) map directly with no or minor modifications, spending-tier permission bits already exist in the cumulative encoding, and the MVP reuses existing proveHandshake/verifyHandshake flows.
rationale.feasibility: MVP is ~7 days of SDK wrappers, a spec profile, and test vectors with high reuse of existing primitives, though the lack of any real merchant integration partner or payment rail makes real-world validation uncertain.

mapped_primitives.usage: Human proves they are a unique, enrolled member without revealing identity to the merchant. The nullifier (scoped to the merchant or purchase session) prevents double-spending while preserving unlinkability across merchants. Nonce binding ties the proof to the specific transaction.

Agent proves it holds a valid credential with the required spending-tier permission bits set (FINANCIAL_SMALL bit 2, FINANCIAL_MEDIUM bit 3, or FINANCIAL_UNLIMITED bit 4) via selective disclosure against requiredScopeMask. Expiry enforcement ensures the credential is still live at purchase time. The merchant verifies the agent is authorized for the tier without seeing the full bitmask or operator identity.

Enables the human→agent authorization chain that Eco currently handles via behavioral scoring. The human delegates a spending scope to the agent with scope narrowing (e.g., only FINANCIAL_SMALL) and expiry narrowing (e.g., 24-hour window). Sub-delegation supports agent→sub-agent purchase workflows (max 3 hops). This is the core replacement for behavioral trust scoring — cryptographic proof of authorization replaces probabilistic trust.

On-chain atomic handshake verification lets the merchant (or payment processor) verify both the human uniqueness proof and agent policy proof in a single transaction. Delegation nullifier replay protection prevents reuse of revoked delegation tokens. Root history buffers accommodate slight latency between tree updates and proof generation during purchase flows.

proveHandshake() generates the human+agent proof bundle the merchant needs. verifyHandshake() runs on the merchant/processor side. permissionsToBitmask() maps spending tiers to the correct cumulative bits. The existing error classes (ExpiredCredentialError, ScopeEscalationError, InvalidPermissionError) already cover the failure modes of a purchase auth flow.

new_components_needed.description: Spending-tier helper module: maps currency amounts to required permission bits (e.g., $50 → FINANCIAL_SMALL, $5000 → FINANCIAL_MEDIUM), generates merchant-scoped nullifiers, and wraps the handshake into a PurchaseAuthRequest/PurchaseAuthResponse envelope that payment processors can integrate against.

Purchase authentication profile for draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth: defines the merchant scope format, spending-tier-to-bitmask mapping, transaction nonce derivation, and the expected verification flow for payment processors. Extends the existing IETF draft with an application profile section.

Payment processor adapter (Stripe Connect / merchant gateway bridge): translates Bolyra handshake proofs into payment authorization signals. The adapter sits between the agent's SDK call and the processor's charge API, attaching the verified proof as metadata. Needs webhook handling for async settlement flows.

SpendingEscrow or PaymentChannel extension to IdentityRegistry: optional on-chain component that locks funds against a verified delegation proof and releases on merchant confirmation. Not strictly required if operating off-chain with a trusted processor, but needed for fully trustless commerce flows on Base.

positioning_alignment: Directly reinforces the privacy-layer-under-incumbents strategy. Eco and similar agent-commerce platforms need identity verification but don't want to build ZKP infrastructure. Bolyra slots in as the cryptographic trust layer beneath their existing purchase flow — the merchant keeps their payment processor, the agent platform keeps their UX, but behavioral scoring is replaced by a Bolyra handshake proof. This is the same pattern as the OpenClaw integration: Bolyra doesn't compete with Eco on agent commerce, it becomes the invisible proof layer that makes their authorization claims cryptographically verifiable. The spending-tier mapping to cumulative bits is a wedge into fintech agent auth broadly — any platform doing tiered agent spending (not just Eco) can adopt the same permission encoding.

deliverables.name: spending-tier helpers
deliverables.description: Module in @bolyra/sdk that maps currency amounts to permission bits (amountToScopeMask), generates merchant-scoped nullifiers (merchantNullifier(secret, merchantId)), and exports PurchaseAuthRequest/PurchaseAuthResponse TypeScript types

deliverables.name: purchase auth wrapper
deliverables.description: Thin wrapper: provePurchaseAuth(human, agent, merchantId, amount) calls proveHandshake internally with merchant-scoped nonce, returns PurchaseAuthResponse. verifyPurchaseAuth on merchant side calls verifyHandshake plus tier check
deliverables.reuses: proveHandshake, verifyHandshake, AgentPolicy circuit, Delegation circuit

deliverables.name: purchase authentication profile spec
deliverables.description: Profile document extending draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth: defines merchant scope binding, spending-tier negotiation flow, nullifier semantics for purchase replay protection, and delegation constraints for agent commerce

deliverables.name: e2e purchase demo
deliverables.description: CLI script: human enrolls, delegates FINANCIAL_SMALL to agent with 1-hour expiry, agent proves purchase auth for $50 to a mock merchant, merchant verifies. Outputs proof bundle JSON and verification result

deliverables.name: purchase auth test vectors
deliverables.description: Conformance test vectors for the purchase profile: valid purchase at each tier, expired delegation rejection, scope escalation rejection (agent tries FINANCIAL_MEDIUM with only FINANCIAL_SMALL delegation), nullifier replay rejection

deliverables.name: integration guide
deliverables.description: Technical doc showing how a payment processor would integrate verifyPurchaseAuth: input/output schema, error handling, trust assumptions, comparison to behavioral scoring approach

risks: No web search evidence of real demand — Eco's agent purchase flow may be vaporware or pivoted; validate before building
Payment processors have zero incentive to integrate ZKP auth until transaction volume exists; this is a chicken-and-egg problem
Spending-tier mapping is jurisdiction-dependent (USD thresholds don't generalize); spec may need parameterization that adds complexity
Solo founder building a payment-adjacent feature with no compliance review is a liability risk even for an MVP
mvp.success_criteria: The e2e demo completes a full purchase auth cycle (enroll → delegate → prove → verify) in under 5 seconds with mock proofs, all conformance test vectors pass, and at least one external developer (e.g., from Eco or a payment protocol team) can run the demo from the README without assistance

title: ZKP-Backed Trust Scoring for AgentGraph-Style Registries
description: AgentGraph provides trust scoring (0-100) and W3C DID-based identity for agents but relies on behavioral heuristics. Bolyra's HumanUniqueness circuit could anchor the human-operator root of trust, while AgentPolicy proofs could replace opaque trust scores with cryptographically verifiable permission attestations.

rationale.demand: Web search evidence entirely failed and no verifiable URLs, named buyers, or engagement metrics were provided — the sole source is an unlinked 'AgentGraph discussion on microsoft/autogen' which cannot be verified.
rationale.timing: No competitor launch dates, standard milestones, or regulatory deadlines cited; AgentGraph itself has no documented release timeline or adoption metrics, making the window purely speculative.
rationale.fit: Three core primitives (HumanUniqueness, AgentPolicy, Delegation) map directly to AgentGraph's trust-score, DID, and multi-hop patterns with zero-to-minor circuit modifications, and the SDK already exposes the needed API surface.
rationale.feasibility: MVP reuses existing circuits and the Python subprocess bridge with ~10 days of integration work, but the absence of validated demand makes it premature to invest even that modest effort.

mapped_primitives.usage: Anchors the human-operator root of trust behind each agent registry entry. An AgentGraph node claiming 'operated by human X' can be backed by a Groth16 proof of Semaphore membership, proving the operator is a unique enrolled human without revealing identity. The nullifier scoped to the registry prevents one operator from Sybil-registering multiple trust-boosted agents.

Replaces AgentGraph's opaque 0-100 trust score with a verifiable permission attestation. Instead of 'trust=78', an agent proves 'I hold bits 0-2 (READ+WRITE+FINANCIAL_SMALL) signed by operator X, valid until timestamp T.' Selective disclosure via requiredScopeMask lets verifiers check only the permissions they care about without learning the full bitmask.

Maps directly to AgentGraph's multi-hop agent relationships. When Agent A spawns Sub-Agent B in an AutoGen workflow, the delegation circuit proves B's permissions are a strict subset of A's with a tighter expiry — exactly the scope-narrowing guarantee AgentGraph currently lacks. The 3-hop limit aligns with typical AutoGen orchestrator→specialist→tool chains.

The dual LeanIMT trees (humanTree + agentTree) serve as the on-chain anchor that AgentGraph's registry can reference. Root history buffers allow AgentGraph to verify proofs generated against slightly stale roots (within 30-block window), which is critical for async multi-agent workflows where proof generation and verification happen in different turns.

AgentGraph integration would call createAgentCredential() at agent registration time and verifyHandshake() when agents interact. The existing error classes (ExpiredCredentialError, ScopeEscalationError) map cleanly to AgentGraph trust-check failure modes.

AgentGraph already uses W3C DIDs. The did:bolyra method can serve as the DID for Bolyra-enrolled agents, embedding the credential commitment and Merkle root in the DID document. This gives AgentGraph a DID resolver that returns cryptographically verifiable trust metadata rather than heuristic scores.

new_components_needed.description: @bolyra/autogen adapter — a Python package that hooks into AutoGen's agent registration and message-passing lifecycle. On agent creation, it generates an AgentPolicy credential. On inter-agent calls, it attaches proof headers and verifies incoming proofs. Wraps the sdk-python subprocess bridge.

Trust-score translation layer in @bolyra/sdk: a function like permissionsToTrustScore(bitmask, expiry, delegationDepth) → number that maps Bolyra's discrete permission model to AgentGraph's continuous 0-100 score for backward compatibility with existing AgentGraph consumers.

AgentGraph DID-binding profile: a short spec document defining how did:bolyra DIDs are resolved within AgentGraph registries, how the trust score field maps to verified permission bits, and how stale-proof grace periods work with AgentGraph's TTL model.

Optional: a view function on IdentityRegistry that takes a credential commitment and returns a trust-tier enum (UNVERIFIED/HUMAN_BACKED/DELEGATED/EXPIRED) for on-chain AgentGraph integrations that want a simple read instead of full proof verification.

positioning_alignment: This is a textbook privacy-layer-under-incumbents play. AgentGraph and AutoGen own the orchestration and registry surface — Bolyra does not compete with them on agent management, routing, or workflow. Instead, Bolyra slots underneath as the cryptographic trust layer that makes their existing trust-score primitive verifiable rather than heuristic. Microsoft/AutoGen gets to keep their developer surface while gaining a ZKP-backed trust guarantee they cannot build themselves without years of circuit engineering. The W3C DID alignment (AgentGraph already uses DIDs, Bolyra has did:bolyra) makes integration feel native rather than bolted-on. This follows the same pattern as the OpenClaw integration: let the incumbent own the API surface, Bolyra owns the proof.

deliverables.name: @bolyra/autogen-trust
deliverables.description: Python package (~300 LOC) that wraps AutoGen's ConversableAgent to attach AgentPolicy proofs at registration and verify them on inter-agent calls. Uses subprocess bridge to @bolyra/sdk for proving. Exposes trust_score_from_proof() that maps verified permission bits to a 0-100 score for backward compat with AgentGraph consumers.

deliverables.name: AgentGraph DID resolver plugin
deliverables.description: did:bolyra resolver (~150 LOC) conforming to the W3C DID Resolution interface that AgentGraph already consumes. Returns a DID document embedding credentialCommitment and agentTreeRoot so trust metadata is cryptographically anchored instead of heuristic.

deliverables.name: Delegation chain verifier for multi-hop agents
deliverables.description: SDK helper verifyDelegationChain(proofs[], maxHops=3) that validates a chain of Delegation proofs ensuring each hop narrows scope. No new circuit work — wires existing Delegation circuit verification into a chain-aware loop.

deliverables.name: AutoGen trust demo notebook
deliverables.description: Jupyter notebook showing a 3-agent AutoGen workflow (orchestrator → specialist → tool-caller) where each agent carries a Bolyra credential, delegation narrows permissions per hop, and a final verifier confirms the chain. Uses mock proofs for speed.

deliverables.name: Trust scoring spec
deliverables.description: Short spec (~2 pages) defining how permission bits map to trust scores, how AgentGraph registries can query on-chain roots, and the proof header format for inter-agent messages. Published as a markdown doc in spec/.

risks: AgentGraph is a discussion-stage concept in microsoft/autogen, not a shipped feature — the integration target may change or never materialize
Web search returned no evidence of external demand; preliminary_demand score of 14 is the lowest dimension — this may be a solution looking for a problem
AutoGen's agent lifecycle hooks are unstable across versions; the adapter may need frequent maintenance for a single developer
Subprocess bridge latency (Python→Node→snarkjs) adds ~200ms per proof verification, which may be unacceptable in fast multi-agent message loops
mvp.success_criteria: Demo notebook runs end-to-end with 3 agents, each hop's delegation proof verifies, and the final trust score is deterministically derived from proven permission bits rather than heuristics. At least 2 GitHub stars or issues from AutoGen community members within 30 days of publishing the adapter.

title: Zero-Knowledge Agent Authentication for Visa Intelligent Commerce
description: Visa's VIC sandbox has 30+ partners building agent-driven purchasing flows. Agents making financial transactions need to prove spending authority without leaking operator identity or full permission sets. Bolyra's cumulative-bit permission model and handshake protocol map directly to tiered financial authorization (FINANCIAL_SMALL/MEDIUM/UNLIMITED).

rationale.demand: Web search returned zero evidence; the only source is a single Visa press release with no verifiable URLs, engagement metrics, or named partners actively seeking ZKP-based auth — this is anecdotal at best.
rationale.timing: No competitor launch dates, standard milestones, or regulatory deadlines were cited; the 30+ partners claim is unverified and VIC sandbox timelines are speculative with no concrete dated milestones.
rationale.fit: AgentPolicy cumulative-bit permissions map directly to tiered financial authorization and Delegation handles scope narrowing, but the AmountRange circuit is net-new work and PCI-DSS compliance requirements are unvalidated.
rationale.feasibility: 20+ engineering days for a solo founder with a new circuit, Solidity contract, spec extension, and an integration adapter targeting an unverified sandbox format — high effort against unproven demand.

mapped_primitives.usage: Agent proves it holds FINANCIAL_SMALL/MEDIUM/UNLIMITED bits via selective disclosure — Visa merchant verifies spending tier without learning operator identity or full permission set
Human account holder proves enrollment to authorize agent's spending authority — sybil resistance prevents one human from spawning unlimited purchasing agents across VIC partners

Human delegates narrowed financial scope to purchasing agent (e.g., FINANCIAL_MEDIUM only) with expiry — sub-delegation enables agent-to-sub-agent chains for multi-step commerce flows (browse agent → purchase agent)
On-chain handshake verification gives Visa partners a trust anchor without requiring them to run ZKP infrastructure — atomic verification in single tx fits payment latency budgets
proveHandshake + verifyHandshake are the integration surface for VIC sandbox partners — wrap in a Visa-specific adapter that maps their authorization request format to Bolyra proofs

new_components_needed.description: Visa VIC sandbox adapter — translates VIC authorization request/response format to Bolyra handshake proofs, handles Visa's OAuth token exchange for partner onboarding
Financial authorization profile for draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth — defines how cumulative bits map to ISO 8583 amount categories and how proof freshness satisfies PCI-DSS session requirements
Spending-limit accumulator — tracks cumulative spend per nullifier-scope pair within a time window without revealing identity, enabling velocity checks (e.g., $10K/day cap) on-chain
Amount-range proof helper — proves transaction amount falls within the authorized tier's ceiling without revealing exact amount to the merchant or Visa network
AmountRange circuit — range proof that transaction_amount <= tier_ceiling, composed with AgentPolicy proof to bind amount authorization to the credential's permission bits

positioning_alignment: Perfect privacy-layer-under-incumbents fit: Visa owns the commerce rails and partner relationships, Bolyra slots underneath as the ZKP authentication layer that Visa cannot build in-house without years of cryptographic R&D. VIC partners get privacy-preserving agent auth without Visa seeing operator identity — aligns Visa's stated 'commerce enablement' positioning while giving Bolyra distribution through 30+ sandbox partners. Bolyra becomes the invisible trust infrastructure that makes agentic commerce privacy-compliant, exactly the 'picks and shovels' position the strategy targets.

deliverables.name: AmountRange circuit
deliverables.description: Circom range proof that transaction_amount <= tier_ceiling, composed with AgentPolicy's permission bits. Uses existing pot16.ptau SRS, dual Groth16+PLONK build like AgentPolicy.

deliverables.name: Financial authorization profile spec
deliverables.description: Extension to draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth defining how cumulative bits 2/3/4 map to ISO 8583 amount categories, proof freshness requirements for PCI-DSS session validity, and amount-range proof composition.

deliverables.name: Amount-range proof SDK helper
deliverables.description: proveAmountInRange(agentCredential, amount, tierCeiling) in @bolyra/sdk — generates composed proof binding AgentPolicy permission bits to the AmountRange circuit. Returns single bundled proof for verifier.

deliverables.name: VIC sandbox adapter
deliverables.description: Thin TypeScript adapter translating Visa VIC authorization request format to Bolyra handshake + amount-range proofs and mapping verification results back to VIC response format. OAuth stub for partner onboarding.

deliverables.name: Spending-limit accumulator contract
deliverables.description: Solidity contract tracking cumulative spend per nullifier-scope pair within configurable time windows. Accepts verified proofs, increments counters, rejects if velocity limit exceeded. No identity reveal.

deliverables.name: E2E demo script
deliverables.description: CLI demo showing: human enrolls, delegates FINANCIAL_MEDIUM to agent, agent proves $500 purchase is within tier ceiling, accumulator accepts, second $9600 purchase rejected by velocity limit. Runs on Base Sepolia.

deliverables.name: Technical blog post
deliverables.description: Educational post explaining how ZKP range proofs solve the agent spending authorization problem without revealing operator identity — positions Bolyra's approach vs plaintext OAuth token scoping.

risks: Visa VIC sandbox API is not publicly documented — adapter may need to be speculative/mock-based until partner access is granted
AmountRange + AgentPolicy proof composition increases verification gas cost; may exceed payment-latency budget without batching
No web search evidence of actual demand — the 30-partner figure comes from a press release, actual SDK availability and partner willingness to integrate ZKP is unverified
Spending-limit accumulator on-chain creates MEV/front-running risk for velocity check transactions
Solo-founder 23-day estimate assumes no interruptions from other projects (ZKProva, GeniusComply)
mvp.success_criteria: Demo script runs end-to-end on Base Sepolia: human delegates FINANCIAL_MEDIUM to agent, agent generates composed AmountRange+AgentPolicy proof in <3s, on-chain verification + accumulator update in single tx under 500k gas, velocity limit correctly rejects over-ceiling spend. Spec published as draft-bolyra-financial-auth-profile-00. Blog post drives >50 GitHub stars or inbound technical discussion on the spec.

title: ZKP Approval Gates for Enterprise Agentic Spending Controls
description: Microsoft's shift to usage-based Copilot Cowork pricing with approval gates signals enterprise demand for cryptographic spending controls on agents. Bolyra's financial permission tiers (cumulative bits 2-4) and delegation circuit could enforce per-agent budget ceilings that are verifiable without revealing the enterprise's internal authorization hierarchy.

rationale.demand: Web search returned zero evidence; no URLs, no named buyers, no GitHub issues — the entire demand case rests on inference from a single Microsoft pricing announcement with no verified enterprise demand for ZKP spending controls specifically.
rationale.timing: No competitor launch dates, standard milestones, or regulatory deadlines were cited; the Copilot Cowork pricing shift is real but there is no evidence of a closing window for cryptographic spending gates specifically.
rationale.fit: AgentPolicy financial bits (2-4), Delegation scope narrowing, and HumanUniqueness root anchoring map directly to the spending-gate use case with only minor modifications needed — this is a natural extension of existing primitives.
rationale.feasibility: The delegate() stub promotion is real 6-day work on unshipped code, and the 14-day MVP estimate is plausible but depends on completing the delegation circuit integration that is currently only a stub.

mapped_primitives.usage: Financial permission tiers (bits 2-4: FINANCIAL_SMALL < $100, FINANCIAL_MEDIUM < $10K, FINANCIAL_UNLIMITED) directly encode per-agent spending ceilings. Selective disclosure via requiredScopeMask lets an agent prove it holds FINANCIAL_SMALL authorization without revealing whether it also holds FINANCIAL_MEDIUM or higher. Expiry enforcement ensures budget authorizations are time-bounded, matching enterprise fiscal period controls.

Enterprise approval hierarchies map directly to delegation chains: CFO (human) → department budget agent → task-specific sub-agent, each with scope-narrowed spending limits. Scope narrowing enforces that a sub-agent can never exceed its delegator's budget ceiling. The 3-hop limit fits typical enterprise approval chains (executive → manager → agent). Expiry narrowing ensures delegated budget windows cannot exceed the parent authorization period.

Anchors the root of every spending authorization chain to a unique human approver. Nullifier prevents a single human from creating duplicate approval trees to circumvent aggregate spending limits. Scope-specific nullifiers let the same human authorize separate budget pools (e.g., marketing vs. engineering) without cross-linkability.

On-chain delegation verification with chain-state tracking enforces that every spending authorization traces back to a registered human root. Delegation nullifier replay protection prevents reuse of expired or revoked budget authorizations. Hop count enforcement (max 3) bounds the approval chain depth.

createAgentCredential with permission bitmask and expiry covers the basic credential issuance. validateCumulativeBitEncoding ensures financial tier consistency. delegate() stub is the primary gap — needs full implementation for this use case to work end-to-end.

new_components_needed.description: BudgetCommitment circuit: extends AgentPolicy with a numeric spending-cap field (not just tier bits). Proves that a requested transaction amount is ≤ the agent's committed cap without revealing the cap itself. Requires range checks on the amount and cap values, plus a Poseidon commitment binding the cap to the credential. This addresses the gap between coarse 3-tier permissions and the continuous budget ceilings enterprises need.

SpendingLedger contract: on-chain cumulative spend tracker per delegation-nullifier. Accepts verified proofs and increments a running total, rejecting transactions that would exceed the committed cap. Needs atomic interaction with IdentityRegistry for delegation chain validation. Does not reveal the cap itself — only enforces the ZKP inequality proof.

Full delegate() implementation (currently a v0.3 stub). Must support chain construction, scope narrowing with financial tier constraints, and serialization of delegation tokens for enterprise API integration. Also needs a new createBudgetCredential() helper that wraps the BudgetCommitment circuit.

Copilot/M365 adapter: middleware that intercepts Copilot Cowork API calls, extracts the spending intent, and gates execution on a Bolyra proof verification. Likely an Azure Function or M365 connector that calls verifyHandshake + budget proof before forwarding the agent action. Requires Microsoft partner API access.

Extension to draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 covering budget-bound credentials: defines the wire format for spending-cap commitments, the cumulative-spend verification flow, and the interaction between budget proofs and delegation chain proofs. Needed for enterprise procurement review.

positioning_alignment: This is a textbook privacy-layer-under-incumbents play. Microsoft owns the Copilot surface and the enterprise relationship; Bolyra slots underneath as the cryptographic enforcement layer that Microsoft cannot credibly build itself (ZKP expertise is not their core, and enterprises distrust the platform provider to also be the policy enforcer). The value proposition is: 'Your spending controls are verifiable by any auditor without revealing your internal org chart or budget allocations to Microsoft, your AI vendors, or counterparties.' This mirrors the OAuth-under-platforms pattern but with a privacy moat — RFC 7662 introspection exposes authorization details to the resource server, while Bolyra proofs reveal only that the authorization is valid. Enterprise CFOs get auditability (on-chain spend ledger) without transparency to vendors (ZKP hides the cap and approval chain). The GREY_ZONE EAD classification is correct: this needs the BudgetCommitment circuit and delegate() to ship, but no speculative research — it is engineering execution on proven primitives.

deliverables.name: delegate() SDK implementation
deliverables.description: Promote the v0.3 delegate() stub in @bolyra/sdk to a full implementation: build delegation proof, verify delegation chain (up to 3 hops), enforce scope narrowing and expiry narrowing. Wire through to the existing Delegation circuit's witness generation and proof pipeline.

deliverables.name: Spending-gate delegation example
deliverables.description: End-to-end Node script: human root (HumanUniqueness) → budget-owner agent (FINANCIAL_MEDIUM, bits 2+3) → task agent (FINANCIAL_SMALL, bit 2 only). Proves the task agent can authorize a <$100 spend without revealing the parent's $10K ceiling. Uses existing 3-tier permission bits — no new circuit.

deliverables.name: Delegation chain integration tests
deliverables.description: Mocha tests covering: valid 3-hop financial delegation, scope-widening rejection, expiry-widening rejection, nullifier replay rejection, and cross-tier cumulative-bit validation. Fast (witness-only) and slow (full proof) variants.

deliverables.name: Enterprise spending controls spec section
deliverables.description: New section in draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth adding normative language for financial delegation chains: tier semantics, scope-narrowing invariant for financial bits, and recommended chain depths for enterprise approval hierarchies.

deliverables.name: Spending controls technical blog post
deliverables.description: Technical explainer: how cumulative-bit financial permissions + delegation scope narrowing solve agentic spending controls without a new circuit. Positions the 3-tier system as phase 1, continuous budget caps (BudgetCommitment circuit) as phase 2.

risks: No validated demand — web search evidence failed and the opportunity is extrapolated from Microsoft pricing changes, not observed enterprise buyer behavior
delegate() implementation may surface undiscovered issues in the Delegation circuit's witness generation that extend the 6-day estimate

The 3-tier permission model (< $100 / < $10K / unlimited) is too coarse for real enterprise budgets — this MVP explicitly defers continuous budget caps (BudgetCommitment circuit) which is the hard differentiator
Copilot Cowork approval gates may ship with a proprietary solution that makes third-party ZKP controls irrelevant before Bolyra can reach enterprises
15 days is substantial for a solo founder — opportunity cost against protocol-autoresearch and differentiation-autoresearch loops that are actively producing results
mvp.success_criteria: delegate() works end-to-end with full proofs: the spending-gate demo generates a valid 3-hop delegation chain where a FINANCIAL_SMALL sub-agent's proof verifies on-chain against the IdentityRegistry, and all integration tests pass in both fast and slow modes. The spec section survives self-review against the existing normative language. Blog post is published.
